questions.question:
Saturn is __________ to be seen on a clear night with the naked eye.

A. enough bright  B. bright enough  C. as enough bright  D. bright as enough
questions.to_find: correct adjective-adverb order to fill the blank

understand.plain: The sentence describes Saturn being visible to the naked eye. We need to fill the blank with the proper English phrasing. In standard English, 'bright enough' is the correct word order: adjective first, then 'enough'. Placing 'enough' before the adjective (as in 'enough bright') is ungrammatical in modern English.

keywords.explain: As a post-modifier of an adjective, 'enough' always follows the adjective it modifies (bright enough, tall enough, fast enough).
keywords.example: She is smart enough to solve the puzzle.

keywords.explain: A word or phrase that comes AFTER the word it modifies. 'Enough' is a post-modifier for adjectives and adverbs.
keywords.example: He runs fast enough to catch the bus.

understand.visual_alt: Adjective + enough pattern shown with Saturn example.

given.aim: Fill the blank with the phrase that makes the sentence grammatically correct in standard English.

terms.meaning: The correct English structure places 'enough' after the adjective it qualifies.
terms.example: bright enough, loud enough, warm enough
terms.connects: Directly determines the answer as option B.

terms.meaning: Placing 'enough' before an adjective (enough bright) is non-standard and ungrammatical in English.
terms.connects: Rules out options A and C.

terms.meaning: This construction does not exist in standard English; it mixes two different comparative structures.

given.plan: Apply the rule that 'enough' follows the adjective it modifies. Eliminate options where 'enough' appears before the adjective or in a non-standard position.

steps.title: Recall the adjective + enough rule
steps.apply: In English, 'enough' post-modifies adjectives. So 'bright enough' is correct; 'enough bright' is not.
steps.note: This rule applies universally: tall enough, good enough, etc.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A: 'enough bright' - wrong order. C: 'as enough bright' - completely non-standard. D: 'bright as enough' - mixes structures. Only B 'bright enough' follows the standard rule.
steps.note: The full sentence reads: 'Saturn is bright enough to be seen on a clear night with the naked eye.' - perfectly natural.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; this question tests knowledge of English grammar rules for adjective post-modification with 'enough'.

questions.question:
Raman is confident of speaking English _______ six months as he has been practising regularly _______ the last three weeks.

A. during, for  B. for, since  C. for, in  D. within, for
questions.to_find: correct pair of prepositions to fill both blanks

understand.plain: The first blank refers to achieving a goal (speaking English fluently) within a bounded future period of six months. The second blank describes how long Raman has been practising up to now, spanning a duration of three weeks. Choosing the right preposition depends on distinguishing 'within' (before the end of a period), 'for' (duration), 'since' (from a point in time), and 'in' (during a period or at the end of a period).

keywords.explain: 'Within' means before the end of a time span, pointing to a deadline. 'I'll finish within two days' means before two days are over.
keywords.example: She will learn the skill within a month.

keywords.explain: 'For' indicates a length of time over which something happens: 'for three weeks', 'for six months'.
keywords.example: He has been practising for three weeks.

keywords.explain: 'Since' marks a starting point in time (a date or event), not a duration.
keywords.example: She has been practising since Monday.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing 'within six months' as a future deadline and 'for the last three weeks' as a past duration.

given.aim: Select the preposition pair that correctly expresses (1) a future deadline and (2) a past duration.

terms.meaning: Raman is confident of achieving something 'by the end of six months' - this is a future time limit.
terms.example: 'Within six months' = before six months are over.

terms.meaning: He has been practising for a span/duration of three weeks - this is a length of time, not a point in time.
terms.example: 'For the last three weeks' = during a span of three weeks.

terms.meaning: 'During' refers to something happening throughout a period; 'within' sets a deadline boundary.
terms.example: During the exam (throughout it) vs within the exam period (before it ends).

given.plan: Check each blank independently: blank 1 needs a preposition showing future time boundary (within); blank 2 needs a preposition showing past duration (for). Then match with option D.

steps.title: Analyse the first blank
steps.apply: Raman is confident he will achieve fluency 'within six months' - meaning before six months expire. 'Within' correctly expresses a time boundary/deadline. 'During' would be unusual here; 'for' implies ongoing state rather than a deadline.
steps.note: Option A and B use 'during' and 'for' respectively for blank 1 - both less precise than 'within' here.

steps.title: Analyse the second blank
steps.apply: He has been practising regularly 'for the last three weeks' - a duration. 'For' is correct. 'Since' would require a point in time ('since Monday'), not a duration ('since three weeks' is wrong). 'In' does not fit a continuous action.
steps.note: Option D (within, for) satisfies both constraints: deadline + duration.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; this question tests preposition usage for time expressions in English.

questions.question:
Consider the following sentences:
i. Everybody in the class is prepared for the exam.
ii. Babu invited Danish to his home because he enjoys playing chess.

Which of the following is the CORRECT observation about the above two sentences?

A. (i) is grammatically correct and (ii) is unambiguous
B. (i) is grammatically incorrect and (ii) is unambiguous
C. (i) is grammatically correct and (ii) is ambiguous
D. (i) is grammatically incorrect and (ii) is ambiguous
questions.answer: C. (i) is grammatically correct and (ii) is ambiguous
questions.to_find: correct grammatical assessment and ambiguity status of both sentences

understand.plain: Sentence (i) uses 'everybody' which is a singular indefinite pronoun, so 'is' is the correct verb - the sentence is grammatically correct. Sentence (ii) is grammatically correct but ambiguous because the pronoun 'he' can refer to either Babu or Danish - we cannot tell who enjoys playing chess.

keywords.explain: These are singular indefinite pronouns and always take a singular verb ('is', not 'are'). 'Everybody is ready' is correct; 'Everybody are ready' is wrong.
keywords.example: Everybody in the team is happy.

keywords.explain: When a pronoun (like 'he', 'she', 'they') could refer to more than one noun in the sentence, the sentence is ambiguous.
keywords.example: John told Peter he won - does 'he' refer to John or Peter?

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing 'he' in sentence (ii) pointing to both 'Babu' and 'Danish' as possible antecedents.

given.aim: Determine whether each sentence is grammatically correct, and whether sentence (ii) is ambiguous or unambiguous.

terms.meaning: 'Everybody' is singular; 'is prepared' is the correct singular verb form. The sentence is grammatically CORRECT.
terms.example: Everybody in the class is prepared. (CORRECT)
terms.connects: Eliminates options B and D which say (i) is incorrect.

terms.meaning: The pronoun 'he' in 'because he enjoys playing chess' can refer to either Babu or Danish, making it AMBIGUOUS.
terms.example: If 'he' = Babu: Babu invited Danish because Babu enjoys chess. If 'he' = Danish: Babu invited Danish because Danish enjoys chess.
terms.connects: Eliminates option A which says (ii) is unambiguous. Confirms C.

terms.meaning: A sentence can be grammatically correct but still ambiguous in meaning due to unclear pronoun reference.
terms.example: Sentence (ii) is grammatically fine but semantically unclear.
terms.connects: Distinguishes between grammar and clarity - both must be assessed separately.

given.plan: Step 1: Test sentence (i) for grammatical correctness (subject-verb agreement with 'everybody'). Step 2: Check sentence (ii) for pronoun reference ambiguity.

steps.title: Evaluate sentence (i) for grammaticality
steps.formula_raw: Indefinite pronouns (everybody, everyone, nobody, someone) + singular verb
steps.apply: 'Everybody in the class is prepared for the exam.' - 'Everybody' is singular, 'is' is singular. Subject-verb agreement is correct. Sentence (i) is GRAMMATICALLY CORRECT.
steps.note: This eliminates options B and D.

steps.title: Evaluate sentence (ii) for ambiguity
steps.apply: 'Babu invited Danish to his home because he enjoys playing chess.' The pronoun 'he' can refer to Babu (Babu invited Danish because Babu enjoys chess) OR to Danish (Babu invited Danish because Danish enjoys chess). This dual interpretation makes the sentence AMBIGUOUS.
steps.note: Grammatically the sentence is fine, but the pronoun reference is unclear.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Sentence (i) is grammatically correct + sentence (ii) is ambiguous = Option C.
steps.note: Option A is wrong because (ii) IS ambiguous. Options B and D are wrong because (i) IS grammatically correct.

solution.result: Option C: (i) is grammatically correct and (ii) is ambiguous

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; this question tests grammar rules (subject-verb agreement) and semantic analysis (pronoun reference ambiguity).

questions.question:
He was not only accused of theft ______ of conspiracy.

A. rather  B. but also  C. but even  D. rather than
questions.to_find: correct correlative conjunction to complete the 'not only...but also' construction

understand.plain: The sentence uses the correlative conjunction 'not only...but also'. This is a fixed English pair: 'not only X but also Y'. The blank must be filled with 'but also' to complete the standard grammatical structure. The sentence means he was accused of two things: theft and conspiracy.

keywords.explain: A pair of conjunctions that work together to join elements of equal grammatical weight. Examples: not only...but also, either...or, neither...nor, both...and.
keywords.example: She is not only talented but also hardworking.

keywords.explain: This is a fixed idiomatic pair in English used to emphasize two related facts or qualities. The first element follows 'not only' and the second follows 'but also'.
keywords.example: He not only sings but also plays the piano.

understand.visual_alt: Bracketed structure showing [not only + first item] + [but also + second item] alignment.

given.aim: Complete the correlative conjunction 'not only...___' with its standard partner.

terms.meaning: The complete fixed correlative conjunction pair. 'but also' is the mandatory second element.
terms.example: He was not only tired but also hungry.
terms.connects: Directly gives option B as the answer.

terms.meaning: 'Rather than' introduces a contrast or alternative, not an addition. It cannot follow 'not only'.
terms.example: He chose to walk rather than drive.
terms.connects: Rules out options A and D.

terms.meaning: 'But even' is not a standard correlative partner for 'not only' in English grammar.
terms.example: X not only...but even is non-standard

given.plan: Identify the correlative conjunction structure: 'not only' demands 'but also' as its partner. Match to the options.

steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.apply: The sentence begins with 'not only accused of theft', signalling the correlative pair 'not only...but also'. The blank position corresponds to the second part of this pair.
steps.note: Correlative conjunctions are fixed pairs and cannot be mixed.

steps.title: Match to the correct option
steps.apply: A: 'rather' alone is incomplete and non-standard here. C: 'but even' is not the standard partner. D: 'rather than' introduces contrast, not addition. Only B: 'but also' completes the standard 'not only...but also' pair.
steps.note: Complete sentence: 'He was not only accused of theft but also of conspiracy.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; this question tests knowledge of English correlative conjunctions, specifically the fixed pair 'not only...but also'.

questions.question:
The following question presents a sentence, part of which is underlined. Beneath the sentence, you find four ways of phrasing the underlined part. Following the requirements of the standard written English, select the answer that produces the most effective sentence.

Tuberculosis, together with its effects, ranks one of the leading causes of death in India.

A. ranks as one of the leading causes of death
B. rank as one of the leading causes of death
C. has the rank of one of the leading causes of death
D. are one of the leading causes of death
questions.answer: A. ranks as one of the leading causes of death
questions.to_find: most effective and grammatically correct phrasing of the underlined part

understand.plain: The subject of the sentence is 'Tuberculosis' (singular), with 'together with its effects' as a parenthetical phrase. Because 'together with' does not create a compound subject, the verb must be singular ('ranks'). Additionally, the idiomatic expression requires 'ranks as' (not simply 'ranks') when categorizing something in a list.

keywords.explain: 'Together with', 'along with', 'as well as' do not make a compound subject. The main subject remains singular, so the verb must agree with the original subject only.
keywords.example: The manager, together with her team, is attending the meeting. (singular 'is')

keywords.explain: 'To rank as' is the correct idiomatic phrasing when placing something in a category or ranked position. Without 'as', the sentence is incomplete.
keywords.example: She ranks as one of the best athletes in the country.

keywords.meaning: The verb must agree in number with the grammatical subject. 'Tuberculosis' is singular, requiring 'ranks' (3rd person singular), not 'rank' or 'are'.
keywords.explain: Phrases like 'together with X' intervening between subject and verb do not change agreement.
keywords.example: The book, along with its appendices, is on the shelf.

understand.visual_alt: Sentence structure diagram marking 'Tuberculosis' as the singular subject and 'together with its effects' as a parenthetical modifier.

given.aim: Select the phrasing that (a) uses a singular verb agreeing with 'Tuberculosis' and (b) uses the idiomatic 'ranks as' construction.

terms.meaning: Despite the intervening phrase 'together with its effects', the subject is singular. The verb must be 'ranks' (not 'rank' or 'are').
'Ranks one of...' is incomplete. The proper idiom is 'ranks as one of...', just as 'counts as', 'serves as', etc.
terms.example: She ranks as one of the top scorers.
terms.connects: The original underlined text 'ranks one of' is wrong; it needs 'ranks as one of'.

terms.meaning: Option C uses the correct singular form but is unnecessarily wordy and stilted compared to the direct phrasing 'ranks as'.
terms.connects: Option C is grammatically tolerable but not the most effective phrasing per the question's instruction.

given.plan: First apply subject-verb agreement to eliminate options with plural verbs. Then choose the most idiomatic and concise phrasing between the remaining options.

steps.title: Identify the subject and required verb number
steps.formula_raw: Subject + (together with/along with + noun phrase) + singular verb
steps.apply: Subject = 'Tuberculosis' (singular). 'together with its effects' is a parenthetical — it does NOT change the subject. Therefore the verb must be singular: 'ranks'.
steps.note: Eliminates B ('rank' - plural) and D ('are' - plural).

steps.title: Choose between A and C using idiom and conciseness
steps.apply: Option A: 'ranks as one of the leading causes of death' — singular verb, correct idiom 'ranks as', concise. Option C: 'has the rank of one of the leading causes of death' — singular but verbose and unnatural. Standard written English prefers A.
steps.note: The question asks for the MOST EFFECTIVE sentence, so conciseness and idiomatic naturalness favour A.

solution.result: Option A: ranks as one of the leading causes of death

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; this question tests subject-verb agreement with parenthetical phrases and idiomatic use of 'ranks as' in standard written English.
